questions.question_text: Work done by a uniform electric field 'E' in moving a charge 'q' at a distance 'd' from a to b is
questions.question_html: Work done by a uniform electric field 'E' in moving a charge 'q' at a distance 'd' from a to b is
questions.explanation: Force F = qE and the work done = F.d = qEd

questions.question_text: Electric potential energy of two point charges q and {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: Electric potential energy of two point charges q and {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Potential energy of a system of charges is the work done in assembling the charges from infinity to their respective positions. Force between two charges q and q 0 placed at a distance 'r' apart is {tex}F = \frac{{{q_0}q}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}{/tex} Potential energy of the charges is given by {tex}U=\int\limits_\infty^r-F\;dr=\int\limits_\infty^r-\frac{q_0q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0r^2}\;dr=\frac{qq_0}{4\pi\varepsilon_0r}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of the following is correct for energy of a dipole in a uniform electric field?
questions.question_html: Which of the following is correct for energy of a dipole in a uniform electric field?

options.text: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases
options.html: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases

options.text: It only aligns itself along the electric field
options.html: It only aligns itself along the electric field

questions.correct_option_text: Dipole aligns along electric field and its energy decreases
questions.explanation: The potential energy of an electric dipole at any point in uniform electric field is {tex}U=-pE\cos\theta{/tex} When the dipole aligns itself along electric field, it gains low energy state during this process due to which its energy decreases.

questions.question_text: If a charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is there in an electric field caused by several point charges {tex}{{\rm{q}}_i}{/tex} . The potential energy of {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is given by
questions.question_html: If a charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is there in an electric field caused by several point charges {tex}{{\rm{q}}_i}{/tex}. The potential energy of {tex}{{\rm{q}}_0}{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation: Potential energy of charge q 0 placed at a distance r 1 in a field created by a single charge q 1 is {tex}{U _1} = \frac{{{q_1}{q_0}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_1}}}{/tex} In a field created by multiple charges q 1 , q 2 ,q 3 ,…q i , the potential energy of the charge q 0 is the scalar sum of potential energies due to each of the charges q 1 , q 2 , q 3 ,…q i . As electrostatic potential energy is a scalar quantity. Thus, the total potential energy of the charge q 0 is {tex}U = \sum\limits_1^i {\frac{{{q_i}{q_0}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_i}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \Sigma\frac{q_iq_0}{r}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Electric Potential V at a point in an electrical field is
questions.question_html: Electric Potential V at a point in an electrical field is

options.text: potential energy of a unit charge placed at that point.
options.html: potential energy of a unit charge placed at that point.

options.text: potential energy of all charges in the field.
options.html: potential energy of all charges in the field.

options.text: potential energy of an electron placed at that point.
options.html: potential energy of an electron placed at that point.

options.text: potential energy of a proton placed at that point.
options.html: potential energy of a proton placed at that point.

questions.correct_option_text: potential energy of a unit charge placed at that point.
questions.explanation: Electric potential V is the work done in bringing unit positive charge from infinity to the point in an electric field, and the potential energy U is the work done in bringing any finite charge from infinity to the point in an electric field. They are related as, U = qV If q = 1C, U = V

questions.question_text: In circuits, a difference in potential from one point to another is often called
questions.question_html: In circuits, a difference in potential from one point to another is often called
questions.explanation: Voltage is an electrical potential difference, the difference in electric potential between two places. The unit for electrical potential difference, or voltage, is the volt.

questions.question_text: If a positive charge moves in the direction of the electric field
questions.question_html: If a positive charge moves in the direction of the electric field

options.text: Field does negative work on charge, potential energy decreases
options.html: Field does negative work on charge, potential energy decreases

options.text: Field does negative work on charge, potential energy increases
options.html: Field does negative work on charge, potential energy increases

options.text: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy increases
options.html: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy increases

options.text: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy decreases
options.html: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy decreases

questions.correct_option_text: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy decreases
questions.explanation: Field does positive work on charge, potential energy decreases

questions.question_text: If a positive charge moves opposite to the direction of the electric field, the field does _______ work on charge and potential energy __________
questions.question_html: If a positive charge moves opposite to the direction of the electric field, the field does _______ work on charge and potential energy __________
questions.explanation: A positive charge experiences a force in the direction of the field. As they are related by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm F}=\mathrm q\overrightarrow{\mathrm E}{/tex} If charge moves opposite to field thus force and displacement will be in opposite directions, so work done {tex}{{\text{W}} = \int\limits_{\text{A}}^{\text{B}} {\overrightarrow {\text{F}} } \cdot \overrightarrow {\text{dr}} }{/tex} between any two points A and B will be negative. Potential energy of the charge increases since an external source is required to do work on the charge against the direction of the field, and the work done is stored in the charge as its potential energy.

Electric potential V is the work done in bringing unit positive charge from infinity to the point in an electric field, and the potential energy U is the work done in bringing any finite charge from infinity to the point in an electric field. They are related as, {tex}U = qV{/tex} or {tex}V = \frac{U }{q}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The unit of potential difference as used in electrical circuits is
questions.question_html: The unit of potential difference as used in electrical circuits is
questions.explanation: Volt is the S.I. unit of potential difference.

questions.question_text: Electric potential at a distance r from a point charges q is
questions.question_html: Electric potential at a distance r from a point charges q is

questions.question_text: In a system of n point charges electric potential at a point P having a distance {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} from the charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: In a system of n point charges electric potential at a point P having a distance {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} from the charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Electric potential at a point V is the work done in bringing unit positive charge from infinity to the point in an electric field. When there are multiple charges, the net potential will be the scaler sum of the potentials due to the individual charges because electric potential is a scaler quantity thus {tex}{V_1} = \frac{{{q_1}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_1}}};{V_2} = \frac{{{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_2}}};...{V_i} = \frac{{{q_i}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_i}}}{/tex} And V = V 1 + V 2 + .... + V i {tex}= \sum\limits_1^i {\frac{{{q_i}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_i}}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\sum\limits_1^i {\frac{{{q_i}}}{{{r_i}}}} {/tex}

questions.question_text: For a one dimensional electric field, the correct relation of E and potential V is
questions.question_html: For a one dimensional electric field, the correct relation of E and potential V is
questions.explanation: Electric field is defined as the negative gradient of the potential. The potential decreases in the direction of the electric field. {tex}E = - \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Electric-field magnitude 'E' at points inside and outside a positively charged spherical conductor having charge Q and a radius R are-
questions.question_html: Electric-field magnitude 'E' at points inside and outside a positively charged spherical conductor having charge Q and a radius R are-
questions.explanation: As in a conductor charge always reside on surface thus inside the conductor field will be zero. At outside, the sphere field will be just as due to a point charge.

questions.question_text: If R is the radius of a spherical conductor, V m the dielectric strength, then the maximum electric-field magnitude to which it can be raised is
questions.question_html: If R is the radius of a spherical conductor, Vm the dielectric strength, then the maximum electric-field magnitude to which it can be raised is
questions.explanation: Dielectric strength is the maximum electric field intensity the medium can bear without suffering a breakdown. The electric field intensity has a maximum value on the surface of the spherical conductor, {tex}{E_m} = \frac{{{q_m}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{R^2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {V_m}R{/tex}
